bibliographic_data.title: Refactoring for Reuse: An Empirical Study
bibliographic_data.abstract: Refactoring is the de-facto practice to optimize software health. While several studies propose refactoring strategies to optimize software design through applying design patterns and removing design defects, little is known about how developers actually refactor their code to improve its reuse. Therefore, we extract, from 1,828 open source projects, a set of refactorings that were intended to improve the software reusability. We analyze the impact of reusability refactorings on the state-of-the-art reusability metrics, and we compare the distribution of reusability refactoring types, with the distribution of the remaining mainstream refactorings. Overall, we found that the distribution of refactoring types, applied in the context of reusability, is different from the distribution of refactoring types in mainstream development.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study investigates how developers refactor code to improve reusability and finds that the distribution of refactoring types for improving reusability is different from mainstream refactorings.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors mined commits from 1,828 open source projects to identify 1,957 reusability commits. They then extracted all refactorings executed in these commits and labeled them as reusability refactorings. The impact of these refactorings on code quality metrics was analyzed.
outcomes: The distribution of refactoring types for improving reusability is different from mainstream refactorings.
Reusability refactorings significantly impact high-level code elements such as packages, classes, and methods.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Refactoring is a common technique used to improve software quality. This study focuses on understanding how developers refactor their code specifically for improving reusability. By analyzing 1,828 open source projects, the authors identified 1,957 commits that explicitly mention refactoring for reusability. They found that the types of refactorings used in these commits differ from those typically seen in mainstream development. The study also shows that reusability refactorings have a significant impact on high-level code elements like packages and classes.
